criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the Markov chain formulation of the LLM generation process over token-level hidden representations.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand how the reward function and value function are defined to estimate difficulty.
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the temporal difference (TD) learning training objective.
The agent has read Section 5.1 to check all datasets, baselines, models, evaluation metrics, and implementation details for the experiments.
The agent has read Appendix A.1 to understand the precise data split ratios for each of the six datasets.
The agent has read Appendix A.2 to understand the exact hyperparameter settings and prompt templates for all five baselines.
The agent has read Appendix A.3 to check all implementation details including Python/PyTorch versions, GPU type, sampling temperature, network architecture, and learning rate.
The plan includes modelling the LLM autoregressive generation process as a Markov chain over token-level hidden representations.
The plan includes defining a reward function R(s_t) and a value function V(s_t) via the Bellman equation to estimate perceived difficulty.
The plan includes training a two-layer fully connected neural network to approximate the value function using temporal difference (TD) learning.
The plan includes loading all six evaluation datasets (MMBench, ScienceQA, MathVista, StrategyQA, GSM8K, CommonsenseQA) with their correct splits.
The plan includes implementing all five baselines: prompt, AG, LLMs-Ranking, HaluSearch-Gen, and HaluSearch-Critic.
The plan includes three difficulty-aware adaptive inference strategies: Self-Consistency, Best-of-N, and Self-Refine.
The plan includes ablation studies on the number of inference attempts, Markov order k, and answer extraction method.
The plan includes cross-domain generalization experiments (training on one dataset, testing on another) and cross-model generalization experiments.
The code for formalizing the LLM generation process as a Markov chain has been implemented as Equation 1: H_{t+1}, y_{t+1} = f_theta(H_t, y_t), with {H_t, y_t} as the Markov state s_t.
The code correctly defines the initial Markov state s_0 = {H_0, x}, where H_0 = (h_0) is the hidden representation generated from the input question x before any tokens are generated.
The code for the reward function R(s_t) has been implemented as defined: R(s_t) = Reward(y) if y_t = EOS, and 0 otherwise.
The code for the value function V(s_t) has been implemented using the Bellman equation: V(s_t) = E[R(s_t) + gamma * V(s_{t+1})].
The code for the simplified value function has been implemented as Equation 3: V(s_t) = gamma * E[V(s_{t+1})] if y_t != EOS, and R(s_t) if y_t = EOS.
The difficulty threshold tau is implemented to classify questions: x is classified as 'Difficult' if V(s_0) <= tau, and 'Easy' if V(s_0) > tau.
The code for the TD error delta_t has been implemented with two cases: gamma * Reward(y) - F_phi(s_t) when y_t = EOS, and gamma * F_phi(s_{t+1}) - F_phi(s_t) otherwise.
The training loss L_TD has been implemented as the expectation of the sum of squared TD errors over all sampled responses.
The value function approximator F_phi is implemented as a two-layer fully connected neural network.
The implementation uses the last layer hidden state h_t as the representation of the Markov state s_t when training the value function.
The ground truth labeling for easy/hard classification uses three independent inference attempts: a question is classified as easy if all three attempts produce correct answers, and hard otherwise.
The sampling temperature T for the LLMs is set to 0.5 when generating training data and evaluation responses.
The learning rate for training the two-layer FC network is set to 1e-4.
The code for the difficulty-aware Self-Consistency strategy has been implemented: sample K CoT paths if V(s_0) <= tau (difficult), use direct answer if V(s_0) > tau (easy).
The Self-Consistency final answer selection uses majority vote: argmax_a sum_{k=1}^{K} I(Ans(y_k^CoT) = a) when the question is classified as difficult.
The code for the difficulty-aware Best-of-N strategy has been implemented: sample K CoT paths if difficult, select argmax_k P(true|x, y_k^CoT) using the model's estimated correctness probability.
The code for the difficulty-aware Self-Refine strategy has been implemented: iteratively refine for T steps (y_t ~ f_theta(y|x, y_{t-1})) if difficult, use Ans(y^(T)) as final answer.
The budget K is set to 10 for Self-Consistency and Best-of-N in the main experiments (Figure 3), and K=5 for Self-Refine.
MMBench dataset is loaded with 50% of data as training set, 45% as test set, and 5% as validation set for determining threshold tau.
ScienceQA dataset is loaded using the official training, test, and validation splits, with the validation set used to determine threshold tau.
MathVista dataset is loaded with 50% as training set, 45% as test set, and 5% as validation set for determining threshold tau.
StrategyQA dataset uses the official test split, with 95% of official training data for training and 5% as validation for determining threshold tau.
GSM8K dataset follows the official test split, with 95% of official training data used for training and 5% as validation for threshold tau.
CommonsenseQA dataset uses the official training, test, and validation splits provided by the dataset.
Both target LLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct and InternVL3-8B) are loaded and used for experiments.
The prompt baseline is implemented using the exact prompt template from Table 10: asking the model 'Is this question difficult for you?' with a strict 'Yes' or 'No' answer.
The AG baseline is implemented using Chain-of-Thought consistency with k=10 samples following the original paper's setup.
The LLMs-Ranking baseline is implemented with Difficulty Ranking (CoT, batch size B=8, random split rounds R=5) and Problem Partition (pre-sample size p=4, judge window size k=32).
The HaluSearch-Gen baseline generates reward data using GPT-4o with the exact prompt from Table 11, then fine-tunes Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct to assess question difficulty.
The HaluSearch-Critic baseline generates reward data using GPT-4o with the exact prompt from Table 12 (including critic explanation on first line, score on second line), then fine-tunes the model.
ROC-AUC and Macro-F1 are used as evaluation metrics for the binary classification task of difficulty estimation.
Easy-Acc (proportion of easy questions classified as easy) and Hard-Acc (proportion of hard questions classified as hard) are computed separately in addition to ROC-AUC and Macro-F1.
Accuracy and total output token length are computed as evaluation metrics for the difficulty-aware repeated sampling experiments.
For the open-ended evaluation (Section 6.1), RLHF-V and VLFeedback datasets are loaded and LLaVA-Critic is used as the reward model to score generated responses.
The open-ended difficulty threshold is implemented as: score below 50 = hard question, score above 50 = easy question (with LLaVA-Critic scoring up to 100).
The cross-domain generalization experiment is implemented by training the value function on ScienceQA and evaluating on MMBench without any fine-tuning on MMBench.
The cross-domain generalization experiment is implemented by training the value function on StrategyQA and evaluating on CommonsenseQA.
The cross-model generalization experiment is implemented by training the value function on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct hidden states and evaluating on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct.
The scalability experiment is implemented by applying the difficulty estimation framework to all four Qwen2.5-VL model sizes: 3B, 7B, 32B, and 72B.
The ablation on inference attempts is implemented by varying the number of independent inference attempts from 3 to 6 on MMBench.
The ablation on Markov order is implemented by redefining the state as a tuple of the previous k hidden states, with k varying over {1, 2, 3} on MMBench.
The ablation on answer extraction is implemented by replacing the original extractor with GPT-4o to verify answer extraction does not significantly affect difficulty estimation.
The additional difficulty-aware sampling experiments use budget K=5 for SC and BoN, and K=3 for SR (Figure 4 in Appendix).
The code for the main difficulty estimation experiments has been executed successfully on all six datasets (MMBench, ScienceQA, MathVista, StrategyQA, GSM8K, CommonsenseQA) with both models.
The code for the time cost evaluation (Figure 2) has been executed successfully with 400 randomly sampled questions from each dataset's test set.
The code for the difficulty-aware Self-Consistency experiments on MMBench, ScienceQA, and MathVista has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for the difficulty-aware Best-of-N experiments on MMBench, ScienceQA, and MathVista has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for the difficulty-aware Self-Refine experiments on MMBench, ScienceQA, and MathVista has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for open-ended difficulty estimation on RLHF-V and VLFeedback with both models has been executed successfully.
The cross-domain generalization experiments (ScienceQA->MMBench and StrategyQA->CommonsenseQA) have been executed successfully.
The scalability experiments across four Qwen2.5-VL model sizes on ScienceQA have been executed successfully.
All three ablation study experiments (inference attempts, Markov order k, answer extraction) on MMBench have been executed successfully.
The training time comparison experiment with 400 randomly sampled training examples has been executed for our method, HaluSearch-Gen, and HaluSearch-Critic.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: our method nearly outperforms all baseline methods across all six datasets and both models in terms of ROC-AUC and Macro-F1.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on ScienceQA with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, the method achieves ROC-AUC of approximately 93.09% and Macro-F1 of approximately 79.48%.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on MMBench with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, the method achieves ROC-AUC of approximately 94.15% and Macro-F1 of approximately 80.68%.
The execution outcomes match Figure 2: our method requires less test-time computation (in minutes) than all baseline methods across all six datasets for difficulty estimation.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: across all three sampling strategies (SC, BoN, SR) on MMBench, ScienceQA, and MathVista, our method achieves the highest or near-highest accuracy while using fewer or comparable tokens.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: on the open-ended RLHF-V dataset, our method outperforms LLMs-Ranking, HaluSearch-Gen, and HaluSearch-Critic in ROC-AUC for both models.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: on VLFeedback with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, our method achieves ROC-AUC of approximately 92.16% and Macro-F1 of approximately 75.57%.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: our method generalizes better cross-domain than HaluSearch-Gen and HaluSearch-Critic (e.g., ScienceQA->MMBench ROC-AUC of approximately 89.03%).
The execution outcomes match Table 4: our difficulty estimation method maintains consistently high ROC-AUC (90%+) and Macro-F1 across Qwen2.5-VL model sizes of 3B, 7B, 32B, and 72B on ScienceQA.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: cross-model generalization from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct to LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct shows a notable performance drop (ROC-AUC near 47-50%), confirming that per-model value function training is needed.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: our method's training time is significantly lower than HaluSearch-Gen and HaluSearch-Critic (approximately 1.56 minutes vs 5.32/10.23 minutes on MMBench with 400 samples).
The execution outcomes match Table 7: the Macro-F1 scores for 3 to 6 inference attempts show minimal difference (within ~0.3 points), confirming that 3 attempts are sufficient.
The execution outcomes match Table 8: varying the Markov order k from 1 to 3 produces ROC-AUC and Macro-F1 differences within 1 point, confirming that k=1 (first-order Markov) is sufficient.

The execution outcomes match Table 9: replacing the original answer extractor with GPT-4o produces differences within 1.5 points in both ROC-AUC and Macro-F1, confirming robustness to answer extraction method.
